dataset_overview.title: Dataset Overview

explanation.what: Dataset overview provides a high-level summary: number of samples, features, data types, and target distribution for the Bank Telemarketing dataset.
explanation.why: Understanding dataset structure is the first step in EDA. It helps identify class imbalance (88.3% no vs 11.7% yes), feature types, and potential challenges like 'unknown' string values.
explanation.how: Check for balanced/imbalanced targets, identify numerical vs categorical vs binary features, and understand dataset size. The 7.55:1 imbalance ratio requires special handling.

dataset_overview.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

df.info()
df.head(10)

# Get numerical attributes list (no binary)
num_attrs= ['age', 'balance', 'duration', 'campaign', 'pdays', 'previous']
bin_attrs= ['default', 'housing', 'loan', 'y']
print(num_attrs)
print(f'Number of numerical attributes: {len(num_attrs)}')
print(f'Feature Statistics:')
for attr in num_attrs:
    print(f'{attr}:')
    print(f'Mean: {df[attr].mean()}')
    print(f'Median: {df[attr].median()}')
    print(f'Std: {df[attr].std()}')

missing_values.title: Missing / Unknown Values Analysis

explanation.what: This dataset has NO NaN missing values, but contains 'unknown' string values in 4 columns: contact (13,020), poutcome (36,959), education (1,857), and job (288).
explanation.why: 'Unknown' values function like missing data. Understanding their prevalence helps decide: treat as a separate category, impute, or drop rows.
explanation.how: Count 'unknown' occurrences per column. poutcome has 81.7% unknown (consider dropping or using as indicator). contact has 28.8% unknown.

missing_values.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

df.isna().sum()
df.duplicated().sum()

# Check for 'unknown' string values
for col in df.select_dtypes(include='str').columns:
    unknown_count = (df[col] == 'unknown').sum()
    if unknown_count > 0:
        print(f'{col}: {unknown_count} unknown ({unknown_count/len(df)*100:.2f}%)')

numerical_distributions.title: Numerical Feature Distributions

explanation.what: Distribution analysis shows how the 6 numerical features are spread. Includes histograms with KDE overlays and box plots with hue by target variable.
explanation.why: Understanding distributions helps choose preprocessing. Heavy skewness in balance, duration, pdays suggests log transformation may help.
explanation.how: Look for shape, outliers, and range. balance (mean 1362 vs median 448) is right-skewed. pdays has median -1 (never contacted).

numerical_distributions.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

num_attrs= ['age', 'balance', 'duration', 'campaign', 'pdays', 'previous']

# Subplot division
sp_col = 3
sp_row = len(num_attrs) // sp_col + (len(num_attrs) % sp_col > 0)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=sp_row, ncols=sp_col, figsize=(18, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Distribution of Numerical Features', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
for (idx, col) in enumerate(num_attrs):
    ax = axes[idx]
    sns.histplot(data=df, x= col, kde= True, ax=ax, hue= df['y'])
    ax.set_title(col.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(col.replace('_', ' ').title())
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
for j in range(len(num_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

#Box plots for outlier detection
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=sp_row, ncols=sp_col, figsize=(18, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Box Plots - Outlier Detection', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')

for idx, col in enumerate(num_attrs):
    ax = axes[idx]
    sns.boxplot(y=df[col], ax=ax, hue= df['y'])
    ax.set_title(col.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
for j in range(len(num_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

binary_distributions.title: Binary Feature Distributions

explanation.what: Binary features (default, housing, loan, y) have only two values: yes/no. Visualized with countplots grouped by target.
explanation.why: Understanding binary feature distributions reveals imbalance within features. 'default' is extremely imbalanced (98.2% no).
explanation.how: Count plot for each binary feature with hue=y. Check if any is too imbalanced to be useful as a predictor.

import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

bin_attrs= ['default', 'housing', 'loan', 'y']

#binary attribute
for attr in bin_attrs:
    print(f'{attr}:')
    print(df[attr].value_counts())
sp_col = 3
sp_row = len(bin_attrs) // sp_col + (len(bin_attrs) % sp_col > 0)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=sp_row, ncols=sp_col, figsize=(18, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Distribution of Binary Features', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
for (idx, col) in enumerate(bin_attrs):
    ax = axes[idx]
    sns.countplot(data=df, x= col, ax=ax, hue= df['y'])
    ax.set_title(col.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(col.replace('_', ' ').title())
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
for j in range(len(bin_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

categorical_distributions.title: Categorical Feature Distributions

explanation.what: Categorical features (job, marital, education, contact, month, poutcome) describe client demographics and campaign details.
explanation.why: High cardinality features like job (12 categories) need appropriate encoding. poutcome with 81.7% unknown needs special attention.
explanation.how: Check unique value counts. Low cardinality = One-Hot Encoding. High cardinality = Target or Frequency Encoding.

categorical_distributions.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

#Categorical attributes
cat_attrs= ['job', 'marital', 'education', 'contact', 'month', 'poutcome']

#get value counts for categorical attributes
print('Value Counts for Categorical Features:')
val_counts = [df[attr].value_counts() for attr in cat_attrs]
for i, attr in enumerate(cat_attrs):
    print(val_counts[i])
#plot value counts for categorical attributes
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=len(cat_attrs), ncols=1, figsize=(16, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Categorical Feature Distributions', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
for i, attr in enumerate(cat_attrs):
    ax = axes[i]
    sns.barplot(x=val_counts[i].index, y=val_counts[i].values, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(attr.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(attr.replace('_', ' ').title())
    ax.set_ylabel('Count')
    ax.invert_yaxis()
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    total = val_counts[i].sum()
    for x,y in enumerate(val_counts[i].values):
         ax.text(x, y+0.05, f'{y:,} ({y/total*100:.1f}%)',va='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
for j in range(len(cat_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

target_distribution.title: Target Variable Analysis

explanation.what: The target 'y' indicates whether the client subscribed to a term deposit. The dataset is severely imbalanced: 88.3% no vs 11.7% yes.
explanation.why: With a 7.55:1 imbalance ratio, standard accuracy is misleading. Need SMOTE, class weights, or threshold tuning.
explanation.how: This dataset at 7.55:1 needs stratified sampling, appropriate metrics (F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC), and resampling techniques.

target_distribution.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

#Target value distribution
target_counts = df['y'].value_counts()
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.countplot(data=df, x= 'y', hue= df['y'])
plt.title('Target Variable Distribution', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Subscribed to Term Deposit')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
total = target_counts.sum()
for x,y in enumerate(target_counts.values):
    plt.text(x, y+0.05, f'{y:,} ({y/total*100:.1f}%)', va='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
#print imbalance ratio
imbalance_ratio = target_counts.max() / target_counts.min()
print(f'Imbalance Ratio (No/Yes): {imbalance_ratio:.2f}')

correlation_analysis.title: Correlation Analysis

explanation.what: Correlation measures linear relationships between numerical features. Values range from -1 to +1.
explanation.why: High correlations indicate redundancy. pdays and previous have r=0.45 (moderate positive correlation).
explanation.how: Most correlations here are low (<0.1), indicating features are relatively independent — good for modeling.

outlier_detection.title: Outlier Detection

explanation.what: Outliers are data points that significantly differ from others. pdays and previous have 18.26% outliers each.
explanation.why: Outliers can skew statistics and bias models. pdays (median=-1) has structural outliers that are informative.
explanation.how:
📐 IQR Method (Interquartile Range):
Lower Bound = Q1 - 1.5 × IQR
Upper Bound = Q3 + 1.5 × IQR
IQR = Q3 - Q1

Values outside [Lower, Upper] are outliers. Standard box plot method.

📊 Z-Score Method:
z = (x - μ) / σ

If |z| > 3, value is outlier (>3 std from mean). Assumes normal distribution.

⚠️ Important: Always investigate outliers before removing! pdays=-1 means never contacted — these are informative, not errors.

outlier_detection.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

num_attrs= ['age', 'balance', 'duration', 'campaign', 'pdays', 'previous']

#Outlier detection for numerical attributes
sp_col = 3
sp_row = len(num_attrs) // sp_col + (len(num_attrs) % sp_col > 0)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=sp_row, ncols=sp_col, figsize=(18, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Outlier Detection Using Box Plots', fontsize=16)
for idx, col in enumerate(num_attrs):
    ax = axes[idx]
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x=col, ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(col.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(col.replace('_', ' ').title())
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    Q1, Q3 = df[col].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    outliers = len(df[(df[col] < Q1 - 1.5*IQR) | (df[col] > Q3 + 1.5*IQR)])
    ax.text(0.5, 0.95, f'Outliers: {outliers}',transform=ax.transAxes, ha='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
for j in range(len(num_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
#violin plot for numerical attributes
fig, ax= plt.subplots(figsize=(10,7))
sns.violinplot(data=df[num_attrs], palette='muted', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Feature Distributions with Outliers (Violin Plot)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Value')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
#table of outliers info
for idx, col in enumerate(num_attrs):
    outlier_count = len(df[(df[col] < df[col].quantile(0.25) - 1.5*(df[col].quantile(0.75) - df[col].quantile(0.25))) | (df[col] > df[col].quantile(0.75) + 1.5*(df[col].quantile(0.75) - df[col].quantile(0.25)))])
    print(f'{col}: {outlier_count}')
    print(f'Outlier percentage: {outlier_count/len(df):.2%}')

target_vs_features.title: Target vs Categorical Features

explanation.what: Analyze how the target relates to each categorical feature. Shows which features strongly predict subscription.
explanation.why: poutcome='success' has ~65% yes rate (strongest predictor). Students and retirees show higher subscription rates.
explanation.how: Compare target distribution across categories using countplots with hue='y'. Large differences between groups = strong predictor.

target_vs_features.seaborn:
import pandas as pd
import kagglehub as KD
from kagglehub import KaggleDatasetAdapter as KDA
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

file_path = "Banking_Call_Data.xlsx"
df = KD.dataset_load(KDA.PANDAS, "younusmohamed/bank-telemarketing", file_path)

cat_attrs= ['job', 'marital', 'education', 'contact', 'month', 'poutcome']

#target vs categorical attributes
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=len(cat_attrs), ncols=1, figsize=(17, 14))
axes = axes.flatten()
fig.suptitle('Target Variable vs Categorical Features', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
for i, attr in enumerate(cat_attrs):
    ax = axes[i]
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=attr, hue='y', ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(attr.replace('_', ' ').title(), fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xlabel(attr.replace('_', ' ').title())
    ax.set_ylabel('Count')
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    total = df[attr].value_counts().sum()
    for x,y in enumerate(df[attr].value_counts().values):
        ax.text(x, y+0.05, f'{y:,} ({y/total*100:.1f}%)', va='center', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
for j in range(len(cat_attrs), len(axes)):
    axes[j].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
